Press enter or click to view image in full size

2 min read
Just now

--

If you’ve ever had a legitimate credit card transaction declined for no obvious reason, you’ve met an AI false positive in the wild. In anti-money laundering (AML) systems, this problem is enormous: traditional rule-based detection can flag over 95% of transactions as suspicious when they’re perfectly legitimate, burying real threats under a mountain of manual investigation work.
I recently built a deep learning model to tackle this problem using a large synthetic AML dataset, and the results taught me something I didn’t expect: the more sophisticated model wasn’t automatically the better one.
The Setup
I compared three approaches: a baseline Random Forest, a SMOTE-balanced Random Forest, and a neural network with class weighting to handle the severe imbalance (laundering cases were only 0.1% of the data). Conventional wisdom says deep learning should win. It didn’t.
The neural network caught 87% of actual laundering cases, a strong recall number, but its precision was just 1%, meaning almost everything it flagged was a false alarm. The simpler SMOTE-balanced Random Forest actually struck a better real-world balance. More complexity didn’t translate to more usefulness.
Where SHAP Changed the Story
The more interesting finding came from explainability, not accuracy. Using SHAP, I compared the model’s highest-confidence false positive against a confirmed true positive, feature by feature.
They looked almost identical. Cross-bank transfer ratio: nearly the same. Time of day: nearly the same. The false positive wasn’t a random misfire; it behaved almost exactly like real laundering along every signal the model considered important.
That’s actually good news for compliance teams, in a strange way. It means the model isn’t broken, it’s encountering genuinely ambiguous cases that look like fraud because they share real behavioral similarities with fraud. Knowing that is far more actionable than a black-box accuracy score, because it tells investigators what to look for next: not “trust the model less,” but “here specifically is where human judgment still adds value.”
The Takeaway
Explainability tools like SHAP are often framed as a compliance checkbox: something you add so regulators can see inside the black box. My experience suggests they’re more than that. They’re a diagnostic tool for understanding why your model struggles, not just that it does. In a field where a false positive means a real person’s transaction gets frozen, that distinction matters.
If you’re working on fraud or AML detection, I’d genuinely recommend spending as much time interrogating your false positives as you do celebrating your recall score. That’s usually where the real story is.
